metadata.file_name: 24. Wang et al. (2022). Why do East Asian students do so well in mathematics? A machine learning study.pdf
metadata.title: Why do East Asian students do so well in mathematics? A machine learning study

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses TIMSS 2019 eighth-grade mathematics data for five East Asian economies (Chinese Taipei, Hong Kong, Japan, Korea, Singapore), with student-, teacher-, and principal-level questionnaire variables. The analytic sample is restricted to 21,340 eighth-grade students and corresponding teacher/principal respondents, and missing values are handled via MCMC multiple imputation in SPSS 26.0. The manuscript does not report applying TIMSS sampling weights or any replicate-weight procedure (e.g., BRR/jackknife) in the random-forest regression analyses. Because the authors fit a single-level random forest regression in R (randomForest package) and do not discuss complex-survey weighting, the analysis is treated as unweighted for the purposes of this extraction.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used TIMSS 2019 eighth-grade mathematics assessment data for the five selected East Asian economies (Singapore, Chinese Taipei, Korea, Japan, and Hong Kong). The dependent variable is mathematics achievement (number, algebra, geometry, and data/probability domains) on the TIMSS reporting scale. The model includes 22 independent variables drawn from TIMSS student, teacher, and principal questionnaires, and the authors report that missingness was small (0–5.7%) and handled via MCMC multiple imputation; no additional grade/school-type restrictions are described.

main_findings.dataset_used: TIMSS 2019 Grade 8 Mathematics (five East Asian economies; student/teacher/principal questionnaires)
main_findings.target_variable: Mathematics achievement (Plausible Values; mean across five PV regressions)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Random forest regression with tenfold cross-validation (five repeats). Top 11-variable model: R²=0.49, RMSE=0.71 (MSE decreased to 0.53 for top-11 set).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using TIMSS 2019 Grade 8 mathematics the study leveraged students’ confidence in mathematics, student SES, and school emphasis on academic success (among other socio-ecological factors) to predict mathematics achievement, finding that the top 11 predictors explained 49% of the variance (R²=0.49; RMSE=0.71) and that the three most important variables were students’ confidence, SES, and school academic emphasis. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzed TIMSS 2019 Grade 8 mathematics achievement for 21,340 students from Chinese Taipei, Hong Kong, Japan, Korea, and Singapore, using random forest regression to rank 22 socio-ecological predictors spanning individual, microsystem, and mesosystem levels. Mathematics achievement was modeled using five TIMSS plausible values, with separate random-forest regressions per PV and then averaging the five estimates. Missing data (0–5.7%) were handled with MCMC multiple imputation in SPSS 26.0. Using tenfold cross-validation with five repeats, the top-11-variable model achieved R²=0.49 and RMSE=0.71 (with MSE=0.53 for the top-11 set). The most important predictors were students’ confidence in mathematics, family/student SES, and school emphasis on academic success; the authors also report cross-economy divergences. Limitations include the cross-sectional nature of TIMSS (no causal claims) and the use of single-level random forest despite the nested TIMSS structure (students within classes/schools within economies).
